paragraphs.text: Below is a compact survey of the Fe(II) and Fe(III) ligand systems visible in the SRD‑46 slice we retrieved, followed by trends and thermodynamic rationale. Where numeric stability data were present in SRD‑46 outputs, they are tabulated; elsewhere we summarize the observed species patterns and qualitative ordering. Source: NIST SRD‑46.

paragraphs.text:
Coverage snapshot (from SRD‑46 tools)
- Fe(III) (metal_61): broad coverage (≈406 ligands; ≈1,473 measurements). Edge-case systems with quantitative ranges include catecholate‑type (Tiron), hydroxamates, thiocyanate, azide; also oxine and extensive hydrolysis/polymerization.
- Fe(II) (metal_62): 20 Fe(II)–ligand pairs and 88 species enumerated in the catalog slice. Systems include:
  • N donors: ammonia (ML–ML4), ethylenediamine (ML–ML3), 2,2′‑bipyridyl/1,10‑phenanthroline (ML–ML3), di‑2‑picolylamine.
  • O/N mixed donors: amino acids (glycine, serine, threonine, tryptophan; ML–ML3), citrate (protonated and polynuclear species present), phosphates (includes vivianite solid), aminopolycarboxylates (DTPA, NTA; ML plus hydroxo‑linked forms), phosphonate‑pyridyl ligands.
  • Hydroxide: hydroxo species and one solid entry are present.

claims.text: hydroxo species and one solid entry are present.

paragraphs.text:
Fe(III) numeric exemplars (SRD‑46 edge cases)
| Fe(III) system | Species observed | log βn (range) | Conditions (as reported) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fe(III) + Tiron (catecholate, 1,2‑dihydroxybenzene‑3,5‑disulfonate) | ML, ML2, ML3 | log β1 ≈ 15–17; β2 ≈ 29–33; β3 ≈ 40–45 | I ≈ 0.1–0.5 M; 20–25 °C | Very high stability; O,O chelation; sulfonates aid solubility |
| Fe(III) + hydroxamates (e.g., acetohydroxamate) | ML, ML2, ML3 | log β3 ≈ 28–33 | I ≈ 0.3–0.7 M; 20–30 °C | Strong bidentate O,O chelation (siderophore motif) |
| Fe(III) + thiocyanate (HSCN) | ML–ML4 | log β4 ≈ 9–12 | I ≈ 1–5 M; 25 °C | S‑binding boosts vs azide; vivid red complexes |
| Fe(III) + azide (HN3) | ML–ML5 | log β5 ≈ 6–9 | I ≈ 1.0 M; 25 °C | High coordination number but modest per‑ligand strength |
| Fe(III) + oxine (8‑hydroxyquinoline) | ML3(aq), ML3(s) | Ksp[FeQ3(s)] ≈ 10^−34±3 | 25 °C | Precipitation governs; very low solubility |

| Fe(III) + hydroxamates (e.g., acetohydroxamate) | ML, ML2, ML3 | log β3 ≈ 28–33 | I ≈ 0.3–0.7 M; 20–30 °C | Strong bidentate O,O chelation (siderophore motif) |

paragraphs.text:
Qualitative class trends observed in SRD‑46 coverage
| Ligand class | Representative ligands seen (Fe oxidation state) | Relative stability trend vs Fe(III) | Relative stability trend vs Fe(II) | Notes from SRD‑46 slice and rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strong O,O chelates (catecholates) | Tiron (Fe(III)) | Highest among surveyed | Not covered in Fe(II) slice | Fe(III) is a hard acid; catecholate O donors + chelation and π‑donation give very high β; CFSE minimal for high‑spin d5, so HSAB/chelates dominate. |
| O,O chelates (hydroxamates) | Acetohydroxamate and related (Fe(III)) | Very high | Not covered in Fe(II) slice | Siderophore‑like binding; strong bidentate O donors favored by Fe(III). |
| Aminopolycarboxylates / polycarboxylates | DTPA, NTA, citrate (Fe(II)); EDTA/NTA (Fe(III), noted as common) | High (Fe(III)); strong | High to strong (Fe(II)) | Multidentate chelation (chelate + macrochelate effects); Fe(III) often stronger than Fe(II) by HSAB; Fe(II) still strongly bound by DTPA/NTA in catalog. |
| N‑heteroaryl chelates (strong‑field N donors) | 2,2′‑bipyridyl, 1,10‑phenanthroline (Fe(II)) | Moderate (Fe(III), not highlighted) | Moderate to strong (Fe(II)) | In Fe(II), strong‑field ligands can induce low‑spin, adding LFSE and boosting stability; SRD‑46 shows ML–ML3 complexes for bpy/phen. |
| Simple N donors (aliphatic amines) | NH3, ethylenediamine (Fe(II)) | Lower than O,O chelates | Moderate | Ethylenediamine (bidentate) > ammonia (monodentate) by chelate effect; Fe(III) generally favors O donors. |
| Carboxylates (monodentate/bridging) | Acetate (Fe(III) polynuclear); amino acids (Fe(II)) | Low–moderate (and often polynuclear) | Moderate (ML to ML3 observed) | Fe(III) acetate shows extensive olation/oxolation and clusters; Fe(II) amino acids form ML–ML3, reflecting moderate binding. |
| Halides/pseudohalides | Thiocyanate, azide (Fe(III)) | SCN− > N3−; both below O,O chelates | Not highlighted in Fe(II) slice | Soft S of SCN− binds Fe(III) better than N3−; cumulative β grows with coordination number but per‑ligand strength remains modest. |
| Hydroxide/water | Hydroxo, μ‑hydroxo/μ‑oxo species; FeOOH(s) (Fe(III)); Fe(II) hydroxo species and one solid entry | Hydrolysis dominates near pH ≳ 2–3 | Hydroxo species present | Competes strongly; extensive polymerization/solids for Fe(III); must be treated separately from organic‑ligand stability. |
| O,N mixed donors (phosphonates/picolinates) | 2,6‑PDPA; 2PP6C (Fe(II)) | — | Strong (varied protonation/hydroxo equilibria observed) | Multidentate and hard donor sets stabilize both oxidation states; Fe(III) expected stronger by HSAB, though numeric K not retrieved here. |
| Oxine (O,N chelate; precipitating) | 8‑hydroxyquinoline (Fe(III)) | Apparent high overall affinity controlled by Ksp | — | Solid FeQ3(s) governs; extraction/precipitation rather than simple aqueous β. |

| Strong O,O chelates (catecholates) | Tiron (Fe(III)) | Highest among surveyed | Not covered in Fe(II) slice | Fe(III) is a hard acid; catecholate O donors + chelation and π‑donation give very high β; CFSE minimal for high‑spin d5, so HSAB/chelates dominate. |
| O,O chelates (hydroxamates) | Acetohydroxamate and related (Fe(III)) | Very high | Not covered in Fe(II) slice | Siderophore‑like binding; strong bidentate O donors favored by Fe(III). |

| Aminopolycarboxylates / polycarboxylates | DTPA, NTA, citrate (Fe(II)); EDTA/NTA (Fe(III), noted as common) | High (Fe(III)); strong | High to strong (Fe(II)) | Multidentate chelation (chelate + macrochelate effects); Fe(III) often stronger than Fe(II) by HSAB; Fe(II) still strongly bound by DTPA/NTA in catalog. |

| N‑heteroaryl chelates (strong‑field N donors) | 2,2′‑bipyridyl, 1,10‑phenanthroline (Fe(II)) | Moderate (Fe(III), not highlighted) | Moderate to strong (Fe(II)) | In Fe(II), strong‑field ligands can induce low‑spin, adding LFSE and boosting stability; SRD‑46 shows ML–ML3 complexes for bpy/phen. |

| Simple N donors (aliphatic amines) | NH3, ethylenediamine (Fe(II)) | Lower than O,O chelates | Moderate | Ethylenediamine (bidentate) > ammonia (monodentate) by chelate effect; Fe(III) generally favors O donors. |

| Carboxylates (monodentate/bridging) | Acetate (Fe(III) polynuclear); amino acids (Fe(II)) | Low–moderate (and often polynuclear) | Moderate (ML to ML3 observed) | Fe(III) acetate shows extensive olation/oxolation and clusters; Fe(II) amino acids form ML–ML3, reflecting moderate binding. |

| Halides/pseudohalides | Thiocyanate, azide (Fe(III)) | SCN− > N3−; both below O,O chelates | Not highlighted in Fe(II) slice | Soft S of SCN− binds Fe(III) better than N3−; cumulative β grows with coordination number but per‑ligand strength remains modest. |

| Hydroxide/water | Hydroxo, μ‑hydroxo/μ‑oxo species; FeOOH(s) (Fe(III)); Fe(II) hydroxo species and one solid entry | Hydrolysis dominates near pH ≳ 2–3 | Hydroxo species present | Competes strongly; extensive polymerization/solids for Fe(III); must be treated separately from organic‑ligand stability. |

| O,N mixed donors (phosphonates/picolinates) | 2,6‑PDPA; 2PP6C (Fe(II)) | — | Strong (varied protonation/hydroxo equilibria observed) | Multidentate and hard donor sets stabilize both oxidation states; Fe(III) expected stronger by HSAB, though numeric K not retrieved here. |

| Oxine (O,N chelate; precipitating) | 8‑hydroxyquinoline (Fe(III)) | Apparent high overall affinity controlled by Ksp | — | Solid FeQ3(s) governs; extraction/precipitation rather than simple aqueous β. |

paragraphs.text:
What overall trend emerges?
- Fe(III): Strong, hard O‑donor chelates (catecholates, hydroxamates, aminopolycarboxylates) show the largest cumulative β, with catecholate/hydroxamate examples reaching log β3 ≈ 28–45 ranges (for cumulative β) under moderate ionic strengths. Pseudohalides/halides are much weaker per ligand (azide, thiocyanate), though high coordination numbers can yield sizable cumulative βn. Hydrolysis, olation/oxolation, and precipitation (e.g., FeOOH, FeQ3) frequently compete and can dominate speciation at modest pH.
- Fe(II): The catalog shows robust complex formation with multidentate N/O ligands. Strong‑field N‑heteroaryl ligands (bipyridyl, phenanthroline) form up to ML3, consistent with enhanced stability via induced low‑spin states and added LFSE. Aminopolycarboxylates (e.g., DTPA, NTA) and phosphonate‑containing ligands also form strong Fe(II) complexes. Simple amines and amino acids give moderate ML–ML3 sets, while hydroxo and phosphate chemistry can lead to polynuclear species or solids (e.g., vivianite in the phosphate system).

and can dominate speciation at modest pH.

paragraphs.text:
Thermodynamic reasoning for the ordering and edge cases
- HSAB: Fe(III) is a hard Lewis acid and preferentially binds hard O donors; hence catecholate (O,O), hydroxamate (O,O), and carboxylate/phosph(on)ate classes outrank halides and pseudohalides. Fe(II) is more borderline; strong‑field N donors (bpy/phen) gain ground relative to simple O donors, but multidentate hard donors still bind well.
- Chelate and multidentate effects: Larger denticity and preorganization (e.g., catecholates, hydroxamates, aminopolycarboxylates) increase β via favorable entropy and enthalpy, explaining the large cumulative constants in Fe(III) and the strong Fe(II) complexes with DTPA/NTA and polypyridyls (ML3).
- Crystal‑field/spin‑state: High‑spin d5 Fe(III) has near‑zero CFSE in octahedral fields; donor hardness and chelation dominate. In contrast, Fe(II) (d6) can gain significant CFSE when driven low‑spin by strong‑field ligands (bpy/phen), boosting stability relative to simple amines.
- Edge cases:
  • Azide/thiocyanate: High coordination numbers can inflate cumulative βn, but per‑ligand strength remains modest; SCN− binds better than N3− due to S‑donor softness.
  • Oxine: Apparent “high stability” reflects precipitation (very low Ksp), not just aqueous β.
  • Hydroxo/acetate: Extensive hydrolysis and polynuclear cluster formation (Fe(III)) complicate simple β comparisons and can dominate under near‑neutral pH.

paragraphs.text:
Limitations and next steps
- We summarized Fe(II) systems and Fe(III) trends directly from SRD‑46 catalog outputs. Due to time limits, matched‑condition (298 K, fixed ionic strength) K1/Kβ distributions across all classes could not be computed here. If you want a quantitative ranking (medians/IQRs by class and Δ between Fe(III)/Fe(II)), I can run a targeted pull at 25 °C and a single background electrolyte (e.g., I ≈ 0.1 M) and produce a ranked table.
